concept.title: What Is a DTD?
concept.shortdesc: A Document Type Definition (DTD) is the original XML schema language — it defines the allowed structure of an XML document by declaring which elements, attributes, and entities are permitted.

section.title: Why do we need schemas?
section.p: A well-formed XML document has correct syntax, but that says nothing about whether its content structure makes sense. Consider:
section.codeblock: <!-- Well-formed, but is it correct? --> <book> <price>Learning XML</price> <!-- Title in price? --> <author>29.95</author> <!-- Number as author? --> </book>
section.p: A schema defines the rules for what a valid document looks like: which elements can appear, in what order, how many times, and what attributes they can carry. A validating parser checks a document against its schema and reports any violations.
A DTD (Document Type Definition) is the oldest and simplest schema language for XML. It has been part of XML since the very beginning.

section.title: Well-formed vs. valid

row.entry: Follows XML syntax rules AND conforms to a schema
Checked by a validating parser with a schema
Optional — but strongly recommended for production content

section.p: All valid documents are well-formed, but not all well-formed documents are valid.

section.title: Internal vs. external DTDs
section.p: A DTD can be declared in two places:
Internal DTD — embedded directly in the XML document:
section.codeblock: <?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?> <!DOCTYPE note [ <!ELEMENT note (to, from, subject, body)> <!ELEMENT to (#PCDATA)> <!ELEMENT from (#PCDATA)> <!ELEMENT subject (#PCDATA)> <!ELEMENT body (#PCDATA)> ]> <note> <to>Student</to> <from>Instructor</from> <subject>Welcome</subject> <body>Welcome to XML training!</body> </note>
section.p: External DTD — stored in a separate file and referenced from the document:
section.codeblock: <?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?> <!DOCTYPE note SYSTEM "note.dtd"> <note> <to>Student</to> <from>Instructor</from> <subject>Welcome</subject> <body>Welcome to XML training!</body> </note>
section.p: In practice, external DTDs are much more common because they can be shared across multiple documents. DITA uses external DTDs — every DITA topic begins with a DOCTYPE declaration that references the DITA DTD files.

section.title: SYSTEM vs. PUBLIC identifiers
section.p: External DTDs use two forms of reference:

ul.li: SYSTEM — points to a file by URI (path or URL): <!DOCTYPE note SYSTEM "note.dtd">
PUBLIC — uses a formal public identifier (FPI) plus a fallback URI. This is how DITA DTDs are referenced:

section.p: The PUBLIC identifier (-//OASIS//DTD DITA Concept//EN) allows XML catalogs to resolve the DTD location without depending on a specific file path.

section.title: What can a DTD define?
section.p: A DTD can declare four types of constructs:

ol.li: Elements — which elements exist and what they can contain
Attributes — which attributes each element can have, their types, and default values
Entities — reusable text shortcuts and character references
Notations — identifiers for non-XML data formats (rarely used today)

section.p: The following chapters explore element and attribute declarations in detail.
